steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main purpose of the text. The passage opens with a San Francisco example, then pivots to Reyes's argument that our understanding of walking decisions is 'insufficiently robust' because many competing conditions shape individual choices.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — To discuss the difficulty of identifying a reliable way to increase walking in any given city: Directly captures the passage's point: replicating one feature (storefronts) won't reliably increase walking because the causes of walking are complex and not fully understood. ✓
  • Choice B — To explain why it is challenging to compare the amount of pedestrian traffic in different cities: The passage never compares traffic counts between cities; it addresses why we can't reliably induce walking. ✗
  • Choice C — To describe the effect of a high density of storefronts on any given city's walkability: Storefronts appear only as an example of a feature that may be insufficient — the text does not describe their effect in detail. ✗
  • Choice D — To present a claim about how individuals' decision-making about walking can be improved: The passage makes no recommendations for improvement; it argues our understanding is too limited. ✗
That matches Choice A — To discuss the difficulty of identifying a reliable way to increase walking in any given city.
